coredata.dc:title: Methodology for comparative assessment of battery technologies: Experimental design, modeling, performance indicators and validation with four technologies
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Irujo, Elisa
Berrueta, Alberto
Sanchis, Pablo
Ursúa, Alfredo
coredata.dc:description: An increasing number of applications with diverse requirements incorporate various battery technologies. Selecting the most suitable battery technology becomes a tedious task as several aspects need to be taken into account. Two of the key aspects are the battery characteristics under temperature variations and their degradation. While numerous contributions using tailored assessment methods to evaluate both aspects for a particular application exist in the literature, a general methodology for analysis is necessary to enable a quantitative comparison between different technologies. We propose in this paper a novel methodology, based on performance indicators, to quantify the potential and limitations of a battery technology for diverse applications sharing a similar operational profile. A quantification of phenomena such as the influence of high and low temperatures on the battery, or the effect of cycling and state of charge on battery aging is obtained. In pursuit of these indicators, an experimental procedure and the fitting of aging model parameters that allow their calculation are proposed. As an additional outcome of this work, a general aging model that allows comprehensive analysis of aging behavior is developed and the trade-off between experimental time and accuracy is analyzed to find an optimal experimental time between 2 and 4 months, depending on the studied battery technology. Finally, the proposed methodology is applied to four battery technologies in order to show its potential in a real case-study.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aging modeling
Comparison
Characterization
Experimental design
Lithium-ion batteries

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Parameters sensitivity analysis to experimental conditions
Technology comparison and selection based on self-designed indicators

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministerio de Ciencia, Innovación y Universidades

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by grants PID2022-139914OB-I00 funded by MICIU/AEI/10.13039/501100011033/ (Spain) and by ERDF/EU (European Union), TED2021-132457B-I00 funded by MICIU/AEI/ 10.13039/501100011033/ (Spain) and by the NextGenerationEU/PRTR (European Union), HYBPLANT (0011-1411-2022-000039) funded by the Government of Navarre (Spain), and Lion2H2 (PJUPNA2023-11380) funded by the Public University of Navarre (Spain). Elisa Irujo was supported by a predoctoral contract from the Public University of Navarre (Spain).

This work is part of the projects PID2022-139914OB-I00 , funded by MICIU/AEI/ 10.13039/501100011033/ and by \u201C ERDF/EU \u201D, TED2021-132457B-I00 , funded by MICIU/AEI/10.13039/501100011033/ and by the European Union \u201CNextGenerationEU/PRTR\u201D , HYBPLANT ( 0011-1411-2022-000039 ), funded by the Government of Navarre, Lion2H2 ( PJUPNA2023-11380 ), funded by the Public University of Navarre , and has also been supported by the Public University of Navarra under a Ph.D. scholarship.

head.ce:title: Methodology for comparative assessment of battery technologies: Experimental design, modeling, performance indicators and validation with four technologies

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Smart Cities (ISC), Department of Electrical, Electronic and Communications Engineering, Public University of Navarre (UPNA), Campus de Arrosadia, Pamplona 31006, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Smart Cities (ISC), Department of Electrical, Electronic and Communications Engineering, Public University of Navarre (UPNA)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Smart Cities (ISC), Department of Electrical, Electronic and Communications Engineering, Public University of Navarre (UPNA), Campus de Arrosadia, Pamplona 31006, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An increasing number of applications with diverse requirements incorporate various battery technologies. Selecting the most suitable battery technology becomes a tedious task as several aspects need to be taken into account. Two of the key aspects are the battery characteristics under temperature variations and their degradation. While numerous contributions using tailored assessment methods to evaluate both aspects for a particular application exist in the literature, a general methodology for analysis is necessary to enable a quantitative comparison between different technologies. We propose in this paper a novel methodology, based on performance indicators, to quantify the potential and limitations of a battery technology for diverse applications sharing a similar operational profile. A quantification of phenomena such as the influence of high and low temperatures on the battery, or the effect of cycling and state of charge on battery aging is obtained. In pursuit of these indicators, an experimental procedure and the fitting of aging model parameters that allow their calculation are proposed. As an additional outcome of this work, a general aging model that allows comprehensive analysis of aging behavior is developed and the trade-off between experimental time and accuracy is analyzed to find an optimal experimental time between 2 and 4 months, depending on the studied battery technology. Finally, the proposed methodology is applied to four battery technologies in order to show its potential in a real case-study.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: The utilization of energy storage systems (ESSs) is on the rise. This has been driven by the electrification of transportation, the proliferation of wireless electric devices, and the transition to the renewable energy generation. Different methods of energy storage have been developed over the last century [1], electrochemical and electric ESSs being some of the most promising alternatives. These ESSs include batteries, super-capacitors (SCs) and hybrid systems. Due to the diverse available ESSs, the selection of the most suitable technology for a certain application entail a major challenge. Numerous comparative analysis have been published in literature. Some authors base their selections on technical characteristics from literature surveys and datasheets. An example is found in [2] with the search for the best ESSs for marine applications. In [3], a scalable battery technology for small-scale microgrid is selected by means of a technical and economical analysis. Other approaches use as well experimental testing to evaluate the battery technologies under different performance and aging conditions. Vega-Garita et al. followed a three phases methodology consisting of a technical characteristic comparison, integrated model and aging testing procedure [4], in which Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP) lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) resulted to be the best option. Same battery technology was selected for low-voltage applications (i.e. vehicle starter battery) in a benchmark analysis to assess the begin-of-life characteristic properties [5].

From the above literature survey, it is found that there is not a uniform selection criteria. A tailored methodology is proposed for each particular application and the assessment methods employed to compare the battery technologies considerably vary. Regarding characterization techniques, there exists a lack of standardization to obtain the electrical fundamental parameters [6]. These characteristics are temperature sensitive, and the flexibility of any electrochemical ESSs to operate efficiently at different temperatures is a vital property as temperature variation takes place in most applications and extreme temperatures may lead to unsafe operation [7]. Not only is there a lack of standardization in the tests, but the long testing time and large sample size required to obtain valuable results also pose a problem. The same limitations play a crucial role in aging analysis. Great research effort is focused on aging assessment and its modeling in order to understand how the degradation takes place and which stress factors have significant influence [8].

Different methods for aging modeling are proposed in the literature [9], but semi-empirical models (semi-EMs) are the ones providing an intuitive understanding. They are composed of analytical formulas that fit the relationship of various stress factors to a curve expression for the capacity loss or resistance increase experienced as a result of aging. Many semi-EMs have been proposed in literature. Primarily, calendar capacity loss models include time dependency with power-law relationships and temperature influence using Arrhenius law [10,11]. SOC influence is modeled with exponents, lineal expressions [12] and higher-order polynomials [13,14]. Equivalent to time for calendar aging, the relationship for charge throughput (Ah-throughput) in cycle aging models is often modeled using a power-law relationship [15]. In most models, the current (Crate) is modeled as an exponential increase in capacity loss and temperature dependency is modeled through polynomial relationships [11,16] or using Arrhenius law [17,18]. Dependencies of DOD are modeled with polynomial and exponential functions [19,20] and the influence of ⊘ SOC is mainly included with polynomials [21].

The lack of standardization in selection methodologies and assessment methods, both in experimental design and aging modeling, along with the long testing time and large sample size required motivate this work. The aim of this contribution is to develop a self-designed methodology that combines experimentation and modeling to thoroughly compare electrochemical ESSs. The assessment methods presented are designed to overcome the mentioned limitations and obtain a general tool that eases the battery selection in diverse applications that share operation profile consisting typically of full cycles and are subjected to a wide range of temperatures. Examples of these are residential, portable electronics (i.e. mobile phones, power tools), service robots, remote off-grid self-consumption, UPS and emergency applications. In addition, this work has the aim to develop a general common aging model that allows comprehensive aging analysis. During the entire work, the target of minimizing resources is present so that this study can be easily replicated with minimal financial and temporal impact.

The proposed methodology is validated on four cutting-edge technologies. Among the diverse rechargeable batteries that have emerged, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) stand out due to their high energy density, long cycle life and low self-discharge. Moving away from leading LIBs, Nickel–Metal Hydride (NiMH) batteries constitute potential alternatives with comparable energy storage capacity and cycle life [22]. For higher power demanding and fast response applications, supercapacitors and hybrid systems play a major role [23]. Lithium-ion capacitors (LICs) have attained special attention due to the hybridization of LIBs and SCs properties including high specific energy and power. The performance of LIB, NiMH and LIC batteries has been individually analyzed and modeled. Specific comparisons have also been published, where, for example, LIBs and NiMH batteries are analyzed for electric vehicle applications [22] or LICs are evaluated as an alternative to LIBs for ultra-low power devices [24]. However, to the best of our knowledge, no paper has presented a complete performance and aging comparison among all of them.

This paper is structured as follows. Section 2 explains the methodology followed in this work to properly compare the four technologies and obtain a general aging model that predicts the degradation experience. The expressions employed for the aging model are introduced in Section 3 and the experimental setup in Section 4. The information about tested cells is provided, as well as the characterization and aging tests explained and the test bench described. In Section 5, the experimental results are exposed. The aging analysis by means of modeling is presented in Section 6. After analyzing the results, a comparison is made in Section 7. Finally, the main conclusions drawn are in Section 8.

In order to compare different technologies for the applications mentioned in Section 1, the following methodology composed of two main parts, an experimental one and a modeling one, is proposed. As highlighted in yellow in Fig. 1, the experimental part consists of two main sets of experiments. On the one hand, the characterization tests designed to analyze the influence of temperature on the cells performance. As explained in Section 4.2, fundamental parameters are obtained in these tests at different temperatures, from –30
∘
C
to 70
∘
C
. A wide temperature interval is analyzed covering all possible situations the ESS may reach during operation in the applications. On the other hand, aging tests are conducted to evaluate the effect of calendar and cycle conditions, as explained in Section 4.3. The operating profile analyzed in this paper consists of ESS performing full cycles at Crates around 1C whose ambient temperature varies. Therefore, certain cells of each technology are subjected to calendar conditions while others are full cycled. The experimental design in both sets of experiments is performed within the framework of minimizing the total number of cells used for each technology, but assuring the procurement of conclusive results. The experimental test conditions are chosen for this study based on target applications, but the results and conclusions can be extrapolated to other applications. Moreover, the methodology followed in this study, with the appropriate modifications, can be used for equivalent analysis of other applications.

The results obtained in the aging tests are used in the modeling part, as represented in blue in Fig. 1, to adjust an aging model. An aging model is proposed in this paper to estimate the capacity loss of the four technologies, whose expressions are introduced in Section 3. Common expression are generalized for the different technologies and experimental results are employed to obtain the model parameters, as explained in Section 6. These parameters collect information on the influence of the stress factors involved in degradation and are comparable among the four technologies allowing the evaluation of its influence. Thanks to experimental results and model fitting parameters, performance and aging indicators are developed in Section 7.

This work provides two main outcomes, as represented with green ovals in Fig. 1. Firstly, a comparison in terms of performance and aging behavior of four different technologies based on self-designed indicators. Secondly, a general aging model valid for different technologies that allows a comprehensive analysis of the majority of stress factors involved in the aging process. Following the concern of minimizing resources present in this work, the shortest period of experimental time required for the model error to be sufficiently small is sought in Section 6.2. The agreement between prediction and real experimental values for aging demonstrate the model developed with that minimal period of time is valid and accurate enough for the estimation of the state of health.

The aging model proposed in this paper is designed based on common degradation phenomena which takes place in the studied technologies (NiMH, Li-ion NMC, Li-ion LFP and LIC), operating in the applications highlighted in the introduction. The operating conditions of the batteries include temperatures from 5
∘
C
to 60
∘
C
, storage SOC from 50% to 100% and cycle conditions consisting of full cycles with currents from 0 to 2C. The aim of the model is to allow a quantitative comparison of the aging of these technologies. The capacity loss is used as SOH indicator. A combined semi-EM consisting of calendar and cycle aging is proposed. Therefore, the total capacity loss (
Q
l o s
s
t o t a l
) is the addition of calendar (
Q
l o s
s
c a l
) and cycle aging contribution (
Q
l o s
s
c y c
), as expressed in the following equation:
(1)
Q
l o s
s
t o t a l
=
Q
l o s
s
c a l
+
Q
l o s
s
c y c
.
The expressions of
Q
l o s
s
c a l
and
Q
l o s
s
c y c
are detailed in following subsections.

The state of health (SOH) is the variable used to quantify the cell aging, which is calculated as follows:
(2)
S O H =
C
i n i
−
Q
l o s
s
t o t a l
C
i n i
⋅ 100 % ,
C
i n i
being the initial capacity of the cell.

Capacity loss due to calendar aging is affected by two stress factors: temperature and SOC. The time dependency is modeled using a power-law relationship:
(3)
Q
l o s
s
c a l
=
f
c a l , T
( T )
⋅
f
c a l , S O C
( S O C )
⋅
t
z
,
where t is time and z is the power exponent. Time dependency is multiplied by the expressions
f
c a l , T
( T )
and
f
c a l , S O C
( S O C )
that model temperature and SOC effect.

Arrhenius law is known to drive the dependence of chemical reaction rate on temperature. The irreversible side reactions that lead to calendar aging are also affected by this law:
(4)
f
c a l , T
( T )
= e x p
−
E
a
R T
,
E
a
being the activation energy, R the universal gas constant and T the cell temperature.

Batteries stored at high SOC have a larger deviation from the thermodynamic equilibrium, which accelerates side reactions that lead to calendar aging. A linear expression is propose to model this effect:
(5)
f
c a l , S O C
( S O C )
= a + b ⋅ S O C ,
where a and b are empirical parameters.

The cycle aging model proposed in this paper includes dependence on temperature and Crate, which are the main variables affecting the cycle aging in the studied operating conditions. Cycle aging is modeled using the following power-law relationship:
(6)
Q
l o s
s
c y c
=
f
c y c , T
( T )
⋅
f
c y c , C
( C r a t e )
⋅ A
h
w
,
where
A h
corresponds to Ah-throughput and w to power-law exponent. Dependence of temperature
f
c y c , T
( T )
and Crate
f
c y c , C
( C r a t e )
are included by multiplication.

The Arrhenius trend of the aging side reactions is modeled as a calendar contribution. With regards to cycle aging, high and low temperatures lead to additional effects that are superimposed to those taking place at soft temperatures, such as lithium plating or SEI decomposition. A second-order polynomial expression is proposed in this paper to model these reactions:
(7)
f
c y c , T
( T )
= c ⋅
T
2
+ d ⋅ T + e ,
where c , d and e are empirical parameters.

The influence of current on cycle aging for currents inside the operating limit stated by the manufacturer is not extreme. This minor effect is modeled in this paper by an exponential expression:
(8)
f
c y c , C
( C r a t e )
= g ⋅ e x p
( h ⋅ C r a t e )
,
where g and h are empirical parameters.

The aim of the proposed characterization experiment is to measure the fundamental parameters in order to compare the cells. The experiment includes the measurement of capacity (
C
a
), energy efficiency (
η
E
) and internal resistance (
R
i
). The characterization test, analogously called RPT, is repeated at seven temperatures: −30
∘
C
, −5
∘
C
, 5
∘
C
, 20
∘
C
, 35
∘
C
, 45
∘
C
and 70
∘
C
. The temperature of 20
∘
C
is set as reference. The RPT consists of 3 stages:

ce:list-item.ce:para: 16 h rest to homogenize the cell temperature.
3 full cycles following manufacturer indications (See Table 2).
Galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) in charge and discharge by pulses of Δ SOC = 20% and rest of 1 h.

ce:section.ce:para:
This experiment is designed to characterize the behavior of the cells under calendar and cycle aging. It begins with an RPT at 25
∘
C
in order to obtain the initial capacity (
C
i n i
) of each cell. Then the cell is subjected to either calendar or cycle aging tests. Every 28 days, an RPT at 25
∘
C
is carried out to assess the cell degradation.

The cells undergoing calendar tests are stored at a specific SOC and temperature. In order to analyze the influence of the two stress factors, four samples of each technology are tested at different conditions. Table 3 shows the calendar aging test matrix. So as to analyze the influence of temperature, each cell type is tested at the same SOC of 100% and three different temperatures: 60
∘
C
, 45
∘
C
and 5
∘
C
. As specified in the first column of Table 3, these experiments are named Test 1, 2 and 3, respectively. The influence of the second stress factor, SOC, is assessed through Tests 1 and 4, being the SOC in Test 4 70%.

The proposed experiment is designed to analyze the effect of temperature and Crate on cycle aging. Four different tests are performed for each tested technology, as summarized in Table 4. The temperature dependency is studied by means of Tests 5, 6 and 7. A CCCV charge is performed at C/2, reaching the maximum voltage defined by the manufacturer. The influence of current is analyzed by means of Tests 6 and 8 detailed in Table 4.

The described experiments are performed in the Energy Storage and Microgrids Laboratory of the Public University of Navarre (UPNA). The main equipment of the test bench, shown in Fig. 3, includes two battery testers and a thermal chamber. The battery testers are BTS-4000-5V-12 A and BTS-4000-5V-50 A, manufactured by Neware, with maximum currents of 12 A and 50 A and accuracies of
± 0 . 05 %
and
± 0 . 1 %
, respectively. The thermal chamber allows a temperature range from –30
∘
C
to 180
∘
C
. Data processing and calculations are performed using Matlab.
In this subsection, the results of the characterization experiments are presented. The effect of temperature on the fundamental parameters of the different technologies is analyzed.

The capacities measured for each technology at different temperatures are included in Table 5. For a better visualization, the discharge capacity is normalized taking the capacity measured at 20
∘
C
as reference. The normalized capacity (
C
n o r m
) is computed as:
(9)
C
n o r m
=
C
T
C
2
0
∘
C
,
where
C
T
is the capacity measured at temperature T , and
C
2
0
∘
C
is the capacity measured at 20
∘
C
. Fig. 4 shows the value of
C
n o r m
for the four cells at seven temperatures.

The measured capacities at −5
∘
C
and 5
∘
C
have a reduction lower than 15% for all cells, except for the NMC cell, which undergoes a greater reduction. Less energy can be hold and delivered, which results in more frequent charges required and, therefore, cells suffer faster cycle aging. By contrast, the effect of high temperatures (35
∘
C
and 45
∘
C
) is an increase of 1 to 5% in the measured capacity of NiMH, LFP and LIC cells. These temperatures enhance ionic conductivity of the electrolyte, resulting in decreased resistance, thereby allowing deeper discharges. Extremely low temperatures of −30
∘
C
negatively affect the capacity of all technologies, especially NiMH and NMC, which reach almost 0 Ah. Conversely, the extreme high temperature of 70
∘
C
entails a capacity reduction of 40% in NiMH technology.

Fig. 5 shows the average internal resistance calculated in the GITT test at different temperatures, computed according to Ohm’s law as:
(10)
R
i
=
Δ
V
10 s
Δ
I
10 s
,
where
Δ
I
10 s
is the charging or discharging current pulse and
Δ
V
10 s
is the voltage response generated following 10 s. From −5
∘
C
to 70
∘
C
, the internal resistance delineates a downward trend with a major slope for lower temperatures. The temperature sensitivity to low temperatures notably differs among the four technologies. The higher sensitivity is found for the LIC cell, with a resistance at −5
∘
C
3.9 times larger than at 20
∘
C
. This sensitivity is lower for NiMH, NMC and LFP cells, with scaling factors from 20
∘
C
to −5
∘
C
of 2.7, 2.1 and 1.5, respectively. Higher internal resistance results in greater energy loss in the form of heat, which implies reduction of the overall efficiency. The main implications are greater self-heating and less energy available to be managed by the cell. In terms of high temperatures, the sensitivity of the internal resistance is reduced. Comparing results at 70
∘
C
and the reference temperature of 20
∘
C
, the following aspects are highlighted. Both LIC and NiMH cells show a major decrease, specifically 66% of
R
i
at 20
∘
C
. Meanwhile, the NMC cell is reduced by just 21% of the internal resistance at reference temperature. The reason for this, is associated to the diffusion of ions that is fostered at high temperatures. As temperature increases, LFP internal resistance decreases as well, with a reduction of 28% at 45
∘
C
. However, unlike the other three technologies, it has a pronounced rise at 70
∘
C
.

The energy efficiency (
η
E
) is computed based on the charged (
E
c h
) and discharged energy (
E
d c h
) as follows:
(11)
η
E
=
E
d c h
E
c h
.

Fig. 6 shows the energy efficiency of the four technologies at six temperatures. Given that the capacity results show malfunction of the cells at −30
∘
C
, it is excluded from the efficiency study. The efficiency at low temperatures is decreased due to the reduction in ion diffusion as a consequence of the increased viscosity of the electrolyte.

A significant difference is found between the lithium-based cells and the NiMH cell, as shown in Fig. 6. The efficiencies obtained for the NiMH cell are below 70%, with an average value of around 45%. The low value in all the temperature range for NiMH may be associated to its higher internal resistance. By contrast, lithium-based cells have efficiencies above 85%, for all temperatures. Moreover, the sensibility of efficiency to temperature is significantly higher for the NiMH technology with a difference of around 35% between maximum and minimum efficiencies. The lithium-based cells show a fall of 4%–6% in efficiency at −5
∘
C
. In the majority of temperatures, LIC efficiency is found below NMC and LFP efficiencies with a gap of 2%–6%.
In this subsection, the results of the accelerated aging experiment are presented.

Fig. 7 provides an overview of the results from the four analyzed cell types under cycle aging, numbered from 5 to 8 in Table 4. Fig. 7(a) shows the results of Test 7, carried out at 5 °C., Fig. 7(b) corresponds to Test 5, at 20 ° C , and Fig. 7(c) are the results of Test 6 and 8, at 45 ° C . Two different samples for each cell type are cycled at 45
∘
C
with different Crate, as indicated in Table 4.

Across all of the results, two types of behaviors can be observed. Firstly, the evolution of the NiMH cells, whose state of health shows an initial increasing trend as the number of cycles increases can be noticed. This behavior is typical in NiMH cells and is due to the so-called electrochemical grinding phenomenon, which takes place in the initial cycles. Certain reactions that happen in these cells lead to the expansion and reduction of the electrode volume. Due to the mechanical fatigue experienced by the active material, particles break into smaller grains, resulting in a slight increase in the amount of material involved in the electrochemical reaction. The result of this phenomenon is an increase in the active contact surface between the MH particles and the alkaline electrolyte medium. The electrode’s capacity increases by admitting more hydrogen and, consequently, increasing its nominal capacity.

Secondly, the degradation of the lithium-based cells exhibits typical behavior, with a decreasing SOH as the number of cycles performed increases. Based on the measured results, LIC is the technology with the lowest cycle aging. At 5
∘
C
and 20
∘
C
, this cell type loses less than 5% of its initial capacity after 660 cycles. The ambient temperature of 45
∘
C
accelerates the cell degradation twofold. The reason resides on the nature of the cathode that stores energy through electrostatic adsorption. In comparison to the lithium-ion cells, LIC cells rely less on chemical reactions, which are strong temperature-sensitive processes. When it comes to the other two lithium-based cells, as depicted in Figs. 7(a) and 7(b), the NMC cell degrades faster for those temperatures. This cell type degrades 5 times and 2 times faster than the LIC cell after 700 cycles at 5
∘
C
and 20
∘
C
, respectively. Moreover, at 5
∘
C
and after 700 cycles, the degradation curve takes a much steeper slope which may indicate that the cell has reached the degradation knee. However, at 45
∘
C
, LFP is the cell that experiences the most rapid degradation. With respect to the NMC cell, its degradation rate is 50% faster. In fact, the cell cycled with a higher Crate, and represented with rounded marks in Fig. 7(c), is the first to reach the degradation knee. It occurs at a SOH of 66% and after being cycled 1122 times, as indicated by a black-bordered mark.

In Fig. 8, the calendar aging results for the four cell types are shown, named as Test 1 to 4 in Table 3. Fig. 8(a) shows the results of Test 3, with the cells stored at 5
∘
C
. In Fig. 8(b), the results of Test 2, at 45
∘
C
, are presented and Fig. 8(c) displays the SOH evolution of the cells at 60
∘
C
, which are Tests 1 and 4. Compared to cells under cycle aging conditions, the calendar aging tests results show slower degradation under these conditions, as the cells are not subjected to dynamic material changes and chemical reactions.

Focusing on the cells tested at 5
∘
C
, only the LIC cell suffers a significant degradation, with a capacity reduction of 28% after 5 months. At that temperature, the other cells are barely aged, and their SOH remains over 98% after the same period of time. The higher the ambient temperature is, the faster the degradation for the lithium-based cells is. However, this rule does not apply for the NiMH cell. Represented in blue in Figs. 8(b) and 8(c), the NiMH cell is the one that shows a slower aging rate. After 5 months, the capacity of this cell type is only reduced by 5% and 2.5% at 45
∘
C
and 60
∘
C
, respectively. To a lesser extent, the electrochemical grinding phenomenon takes place and overlaps the aging phenomena, as the cell is being cycled in the RPTs.

A significant difference in the degradation rate is found for the lithium-based cells tested at 45
∘
C
and at 60
∘
C
. The effect of high temperature is attributed to the acceleration of chemical reactions, which results in formation of unwanted by-products, and physical damage of materials due to thermal expansion and decomposition. At 45
∘
C
, after 140 days, just the LIC and NMC cells reach a SOH of 90%, meanwhile LFP remains over that value. As can be appreciated in Fig. 8(b), the degradation rate is similar. However, it significantly differs when the ambient temperature is 60
∘
C
, as can be observed in Fig. 8(c). Directing attention solely to the cells stored at 100% and represented by triangular markers, a clear difference can be observed. The acceleration experienced by the cell on account of that high temperature is larger for the LIC cell, followed by LFP and then NMC. The degradation suffered by the LIC cell after 140 days is 2.7 times higher than that of NMC. Considering as well the cells stored at 70%, the influence of SOC on the calendar degradation can be seen, especially in the LIC technology. By comparing the SOH evolution of the two LIC samples, represented in green, it can be noticed that the cell stored at 100% degrades more than twice as fast as the cell stored at 70% (See Fig. 8(c)).

The parameters of the general aging model presented in Section 3 are fitted in Section 6.1 to the experimental results of the four studied technologies presented in Section 5. The calendar aging model is obtained for the four technologies, but the cycle aging model is not applied to the NiMH cell, as it is dominated by the characteristic electrochemical grinding phenomenon. Including the NiMH experimental results in the cycle aging model would imply also modeling that phenomenon, which is out of the scope of this work, where an overall model valid for different technologies is proposed.

Section 6.2 presents an analysis of the minimum number of RPTs required to get accurate results. The values obtained for the model parameters are presented after this analysis concerning the number of RPTs. The values of the aging model parameters are used for the quantification of the effect of various aging stress factors on the studied technologies.
In order to evaluate the robustness of the fitting method, a sensitivity analysis of the parameters with respect to the experimental conditions is conducted, as presented in Section 6.3

Eq. (3) is fitted to the experimental results obtained in Tests 1 to 4 in order to fit the calendar aging contribution. The effect of the few cycles performed during the required RPT for capacity measurement is neglected, thereby attributing the battery aging only to calendar. The first parameter to be fitted is the exponent z , using the results obtained in the four experiments. The value of z is computed as the average fitting value obtained in the four cells.

Afterwards, with the known value of z , the multiplying term of Eq. (3) is used to obtain the temperature dependency, from Tests 1 to 3, thereby obtaining the activation energy
E
a
introduced in Eq. (4). Finally, Tests 1 and 4 are used for the fitting of the SOC dependency, based on Eq. (5), providing the parameters a and b .

The first step followed for the fitting of the cycle aging performance is to subtract the effect of the calendar aging modeled above from the results of Tests 5 to 8. Thus, the solely contribution of the pure cycle aging to the capacity loss is obtained and used to fit the cycle aging model. Similar to the approach used for calendar aging, Eq. (6) is fitted to the results to obtain the exponent w as the average value obtained from the four tests. Once the value of w is known, Tests 5, 6 and 7 are used to fit Eq. (7), thereby getting the values of parameters c , d and e that define the temperature dependency of cycle aging. Finally, the results of Tests 5 and 8 are used to include the effect of Crate, as introduced in Eq. (8), getting the values of parameters g and h .

The parameter fitting methodology presented in Section 6.1 is applied herein using data measured in a variable number of RPT experiments, ranging from 2 to 7. The aim is to assess the duration of the aging tests required to get accurate results. This accuracy is quantified by means of the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) provided by the fitted models, calculated as follows:
(12)
R M S E =
1
n
∑
i = 1
n
( S O H e x
p
i
− S O H m o
d
i
)
2
,
i being the RPT number and n the total number of RPTs.
S O H e x p
refers to the state of health measured by means of each RPT, and
S O H m o d
is the SOH predicted by the aging model. The larger the number of RPTs used for the parameter fitting, the more accurate the model is. An example of the reduction of the estimation error is shown in Fig. 9, which represents the RMSE obtained in the four calendar aging experiments of the NMC cell.

For the assessment of the minimum number of RPTs, the average RMSE from the calendar experiments (Tests 1 to 4) and from the cycle experiments (Tests 4 to 8) is considered. The threshold value for this RMSE to be considered suitable has been set at 5%. As this threshold is reached, the fitting parameters converges to the suitable values that accurately define the model. Therefore, Tables 6 and 7 show the required number of RPTs, the achieved RMSE value and the values of the fitted model parameters for the studied technologies.

The defined model parameters allow proper interpretation of each stress factor influence. In terms of calendar aging parameters, z shows how the passage of time influences capacity loss, it being almost increasingly linear with a slight square root tendency for LFP and LIC cells. In the case of the NMC cell, the capacity loss rate due to time is greater and the dependency tends to a quadratic. Nevertheless, for the NiMH cell, the impact of time is different and regardless of the duration of storage, the capacity loss remains relatively constant. When it comes to temperature influence, the activation energy reveals the additional energy required for aging side reactions to take place. Moreover, the greater
E a
is, the more sensitive aging is to temperature. The NiMH cell is the less sensitive followed by NMC, LFP and LIC cells, in increasing order of temperature influence. The scale of a and b depends on
E a
, as can be appreciated in Table 6. Therefore, to evaluate the SOC influence,
| b / a |
is computed. This corresponds to the linear function slope of capacity loss dependence on SOC. A greater value means the SOC impact is more significant. The NiMH cell leads the ranking being the most sensitive to SOC followed by NMC, LIC and LFP cells, with slopes of 0.025, 0.023, 0.019 and 0.011, respectively.

In reference to cycle aging parameters, equivalent to time, the power exponent w reveals how Ah-throughput impacts on capacity loss. The influence for NMC and LFP cells is almost linear increasing capacity loss as the cell undergoes cycles. The LFP cell shows a greater increasing rate than the NMC cell due to a more quadratic tendency. In contrast, capacity loss rate increases slower in the case of the LIC cell following a square root function as the cell is cycled. The effect of high and low temperatures is more significant in LIC, NMC and LFP cells, as per decreasing order. Lastly, no influence of Crate was found for NMC and LIC cells, but capacity loss in the LFP cell increases about 50% faster if cycled at 1C with respect to the rate followed when cycled at C/2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameters sensitivity analysis to experimental conditions
ce:section.ce:para:
To in-depth analyze the parameters sensitivity to variations in experimental conditions, a Differential Sensitivity Analysis (DSA) based on partial derivatives has been conducted. This methodology is a local SA type that explores the change of the fitting parameters ( y ) by varying one stress factor (
x
i
) while keeping the others constant [25]. To measure the parametric sensitivity, the elasticity of y with respect to
x
i
is defined as:
(13)
E
i
=
|
∂ y
∂
x
i
x
i
y
|
,
where
∂ y
∂
x
i
is the partial derivative multiplied by
x
i
y
to make it dimensionless, thereby allowing a comparison among different parameters and stress factors. The absolute value is computed, as well, to facilitate the comparison and representation of results. The sensitivity of the calendar aging model fitting parameters analyzed are a , b and
E a
to variations in
Q
l o s
s
c a l
, SOC and T. Due to measurement equipment, error is introduced in the stress factors which may have impact on the fitting parameters. The elasticity results for the four technologies are presented in Fig. 10 along with a plane of elasticity of 2 in purple. More than 70% of the parameters show lower elasticity than 2 to stress factors variations (85% of them present lower elasticity than 1.5). Greater elasticities are found for a and b with respect to temperature. Typical accuracy of temperature sensors is around
± 0 .
5
∘
C
, which means, in worst-case scenario, 0.18% error when measuring temperatures around 273K. Considering the elasticity of an LFP cell which is close to 40, this would lead to an error of 7% in a . The values of a calculated for the LFP cell differs from the remaining technologies, at least in 2 orders of magnitude, making this error of 7% negligible.

Regarding the sensitivity of the cycle aging model fitting parameters, g , h , c , d and e are analyzed to variations in
Q
l o s
s
c y c
, Crate and T. The elasticity results for the technologies are presented in Fig. 11 along with a plane of elasticity of 2 in purple. More than 90% of the parameters show lower elasticity than 2 to stress factors variations. Equivalent to calendar aging parameters, the greater elasticities are found with respect to temperature being their values lower than 10. Due to exposed reasons regarding the high precision of temperature sensors, an error in temperature measure of 0.18% results on no more than 1.8% error made in fitting parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Technology comparison and selection based on self-designed indicators
ce:section.ce:para: Nine parameters are defined in this section to assess a quantitative comparison among the studied technologies. Some of these parameters are performance indicators based on results from Section 4.2, while others are aging indicators based on the aging model parameters proposed in Section 6.

ce:list-item.ce:para:
C
L T
[ % ] – Effect of low temperature on capacity. The ratio between the capacities measured at low temperatures and the reference capacity at 20
∘
C
is computed to evaluate this indicator:
(14)
C
L T
=
∑
i = 1
n
C
T
i
n ⋅
C
2
0
∘
C
⋅ 100 ,
C
T
i
being the capacity measured at a temperature
T
i
and n the number of temperatures considered. In this case, n is equal to 3 where
T
1
corresponds to −30
∘
C
,
T
2
to −5
∘
C
and
T
3
to 5
∘
C
. A larger value indicates more suitability for low temperature operation.

C
H T
[ % ] – Effect of high temperature on capacity. As with low temperatures, this indicator is calculated by means of the same Eq. (14), using capacities measured at high temperatures. Specifically,
T
1
corresponds to 35
∘
C
,
T
2
to 45
∘
C
and
T
3
to 70
∘
C
. A large value indicates a positive effect of high temperatures on capacity, mainly due to enhanced ionic conductivity of the electrolyte.

R
i n t , L T
[ % ] – Effect of low temperature on internal resistance. This indicator is evaluated as the ratio between
R
i n t
at low temperatures and the reference internal resistance at 20
∘
C
:
(15)
R
i n t , L T
=
∑
i = 1
n
R
i n t ,
T
i
n ⋅
R
i n t , 2
0
∘
C
⋅ 100 ,
R
i n t ,
T
i
being the internal resistance measured at a temperature
T
i
. Two low temperatures are considered, specifically,
T
1
is −5
∘
C
and
T
2
is 5
∘
C
. A large value indicates a lower performance of the battery at low temperature.

R
i n t , H T
[ % ] – Effect of high temperature on internal resistance. Employing Eq. (15) and considering high temperatures of 35
∘
C
, 45
∘
C
and 70
∘
C
, this indicator is calculated. Similar to
R
i n t , L T
, a large value indicates lower performance at high temperature.

η
E
[ % ] – Energy efficiency. This is evaluated at 20
∘
C
by means of Eq. (11).
z [Unitless] – Calendar aging rate. This indicator is the power exponent of the time dependency in the calendar aging model. A large value is indicative of a more significant time influence.
w [Unitless] – Cycle aging rate. This indicator corresponds to the power exponent of the Ah-throughput in the cycle aging model. A large value indicates a more significant influence of cycle aging.

E
a
[kJ/mol] – Temperature influence on aging. This parameter is the activation energy obtained from the aging Arrhenius function. A larger
E
a
corresponds to more significant influence of temperature on cell aging.

| b / a |
[Unitless] – SOC influence on aging. This ratio shows the relevance of SOC in the cell aging.

ce:section.ce:para:
Table 8 shows the values of each parameter for the four technologies. In order to graphically represent this comparison, the parameters are normalized considering the intervals defined in Table 8 with the following expression:
(16)
N
i
=
X
i
−
X
m i n
X
m a x
−
X
m i n
,
where
N
i
is the normalized value of the parameter
X
i
.
X
m a x
and
X
m i n
define the whole interval for the parameter. Those intervals are determined based on values presented in the references indicated in the table. The energy efficiency (
η
E
) is physically limited from 0% to 100%. If the parameter is a desired characteristic for the cell, the performance indicator is
N
i
. By contrast, if this is an undesired characteristic, the performance indicator is
1 −
N
i
, so that a higher value for the performance indicator is always a positive characteristic of the technology.

Fig. 12 graphically represents the evaluation of the nine indicators for the four technologies. As stated above, a high value in each of these indicators is considered to be positive for the technology performance. These graphs allow a convenient comparison of the four technologies in terms of the proposed indicators and can be used to select the most suitable technology for each application. Next paragraphs discuss both the comparison and selection among the four technologies.

Focusing on the effect of temperature revealed by indicators
C
L T
,
C
H T
,
R
i n t , H T
and
R
i n t , L T
, the LIC cell shows the best general performance. The internal resistance is significantly affected by low temperatures (with an indicator value around 0.5), but the remaining temperature-related indicators are in the upper range defined in Table 8. The LFP cell is also very robust at low temperatures, both in resistance and capacity retention. However, its operation at high temperature is significantly reduced. Regarding high temperature applications, the LIC and NiMH cells show better performance. These indicators are determinant for remote off-grid self-consumption applications characterized by extreme temperatures or emergency lighting in wind turbines. The comparison reveals that LFP and LIC cells are most suitable for off-grid systems in polar sites [31] or emergency lighting in wind turbines, and NiMH and LIC are best options for off-grid systems in rural areas characterized by high temperatures.

Besides this comparative analysis between the studied technologies and the selection of the proper technology, the ranges defined in Table 8 allow a general comparison with other studies published in the bibliography. For instance, the four studied technologies show better performance at low temperature than the average literature values. However, the performance at high temperatures depends on the technology. The low energy efficiency (
η
E
) of the NiMH cell draws attention compared to lithium-based cells whose value is over 95%.

With regard to aging behavior, the NiMH cell presents a lower capacity loss rate due to the passage of time over the six months of testing, as evaluated through the indicator z. This due to the fact the aging mechanisms are overlapped with the electrochemical grinding phenomenon. In terms of the reviewed literature, no deep analysis on this phenomenon is found so that it can be concluded that calendar aging is slower for this technology over longer periods of time. Comparing the lithium-based cells, LFP and LIC cells present similar and lower calendar aging rate than the NMC cell, whose degradation is 60% faster. Temperature plays a significant role also in aging, and its influence is less significant for NiMH and NMC cells. In average, LFP and LIC cells require 4 times the energy needed by NiMH and NMC cells for aging side reactions to take place. In case of SOC influence, the opposite occurs and the least sensitive ones are the LFP and LIC cells. In applications subjected to calendar aging, such as UPS, service robots and power tools, the NiMH cell is the best option, given that it has a lower calendar aging rate, but also less influence of temperature in aging, as revealed by the indicator
E
a
. Nevertheless, the indicator
| b / a |
shows that NiMH cells are more influenced by SOC in calendar aging. Therefore, keeping a storage SOC lower than 100% would be a useful approach to reduce degradation.

Regarding the capacity loss due to Ah-throughput, the LIC cell leads the ranking with lower cycle aging rate and is followed by NMC and LFP cells, being 70% and 150% faster, respectively. In portable electronics or residential applications, cycle aging plays a crucial role as batteries perform at least one cycle per day in normal use. Indicator w is the one that determines which technologies show lower aging rate and, therefore, selects NMC and LIC as potential alternatives for these applications.

This paper proposes a quantitative methodology to assess battery technologies, based on nine indicators. The performance indicators are measured by means of the proposed experimental design. Besides the comparative methodology, this contribution has as second outcome a general aging model that allows a comprehensive analysis of stress factors affecting battery degradation. The robustness of the model to experimental conditions is studied through a sensitivity analysis. The paper also analyzes the minimum experimental work required to achieve informative and accurate results.

The methodology assesses the temperature effect on the capacity, the internal resistance and the energy efficiency. Moreover, the effect of time, SOC, Ah-throughput, Crate and temperature on battery aging is quantified. The proposed approach allows the selection of the most suitable battery technology for various applications based on a quantitative comparison, which constitutes the main innovation of the paper. In the event of a relevant influence of additional operating characteristics, such as DOD or mean SOC, the proposed methodology needs to be extended to cover these stress factors. Unlike previous publications, this contribution provides standard performance and aging behavior assessment methods in which the compromise between accuracy and comparable results is met. Indeed, in contrast to the specific models for each technology and case study presented in the literature, this paper proposes a general aging model that can be used for different technologies. Moreover, if the chemistry-specific behaviors, such as the electrochemical grinding in NiMH cells or the sulfation in lead–acid cells, need to be evaluated, a tailored analysis can be used to add these phenomena to the general methodology developed in the paper. Both the optimized assessment methods and general aging model proposed in the paper, along with the comparative methodology, are innovations that overcome some of the gaps identified in the literature.

The proposed methodology is validated on four technologies: NiMH, NMC, LFP and LIC. It has been proved that the experimental test matrix, designed with the target of minimizing resources, provides useful results for deeper analysis and comparison between different technologies. The proposed aging model allows a comprehensive analysis of stress factors affecting battery aging. Using experimental aging tests results, the model parameters for different technologies are fitted. This paper provides the interpretation of each parameter, revealing the significance of each stress factor. Moreover, this work analyzes the minimum number of RPTs needed as check-ups in aging tests to evaluate cell degradation. The experimental aging results of more than one year with 32 cells reveal a required experimental time of between 2 and 4 months. In case the methodology is repeated to another cell of these technologies, this minimum testing time is sufficient to provide accurate results.

Thanks to the self-designed indicators, the validation provides conclusions regarding the four technologies compared, which allows selecting the most suitable one for several case studies. On the whole, the LIC cell shows better performance at extreme temperatures, but it is competing with NMC and LFP cells at high and low temperatures, respectively. This positions LIC and NMC cells as the most appropriate ones for remote off-grid self-consumption applications at high temperature locations. Meanwhile, LIC and LFP are more suitable for those off-grid systems in polar sites and emergency lighting in wind turbines. The influence of the main aging stress factors is compared between the four technologies. It is found that the aging of the NiMH cell is significantly affected by its electrochemical grinding phenomenon. Considering the lithium-based technologies, cycle aging is lower for the NMC and LIC cell, which makes them the most suitable technologies for portable electronics and residential applications. LFP and NiMH cells age slower due to calendar, depending on temperature and SOC conditions, and, therefore, are the best alternatives for UPS, service robots and power tools.

All in all, this work provides an analytical procedure to compare different battery technologies, allowing a quantitative analysis of their aging behavior. Consequently, this methodology constitutes a useful tool when selecting an electrochemical energy storage system for a given application. By employing minimum resources, this comparison can be reproduced for other cells and under different operating conditions. In future studies, it would be valuable to apply this methodology in sodium-ion cells. Moreover, an important direction for future research could involve expanding the methodology for applications performing shorter cycles.

Elisa Irujo: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Alberto Berrueta: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Pablo Sanchis: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Alfredo Ursúa: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants PID2022-139914OB-I00 funded by MICIU/AEI/10.13039/501100011033/ (Spain) and by ERDF/EU (European Union), TED2021-132457B-I00 funded by MICIU/AEI/ 10.13039/501100011033/ (Spain) and by the NextGenerationEU/PRTR (European Union), HYBPLANT (0011-1411-2022-000039) funded by the Government of Navarre (Spain), and Lion2H2 (PJUPNA2023-11380) funded by the Public University of Navarre (Spain). Elisa Irujo was supported by a predoctoral contract from the Public University of Navarre (Spain).

Energy Strategy Rev
